title: The Infrastructure Challenge Behind Production AI
gist: A panel of production-AI infrastructure operators argues the hard problem has shifted from building models to running them: token cost is compounding ~100x/year for many orgs, compute and energy (not just GPUs) are the binding constraints, and databases are becoming the agentic control plane while autoscaling and clean rollbacks break down against unbounded autonomous agents.

insights.claim: Aggregate token spend for many organizations is rising roughly 100x per year, far above the ~10x/year that inference operators expected, making it hard to size production systems that must serve an order of magnitude more scale within a year.
insights.speaker: Meryem Arik
insights.so_what: Cost models and capacity plans built on 10x annual growth will be off by 10x; budget and architecture must assume order-of-magnitude scale jumps within a single year.
insights.quote: we maybe anticipated that the token cost would go up, let's say, aggregate 10x every year. It seems like it's more like 100x for most organizations.
insights.locator: Jevons Paradox is one of those things

insights.claim: Token cost is breaking company budgets regardless of company size and is a bigger practical constraint than the widely feared GPU shortage.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Vendors and startups selling token-cost governance/observability address a live, universal pain point, not a niche.
insights.quote: The token cost is increasing, and it's a big challenge. It's breaking the budget of companies, no matter how large or small they are.
insights.locator: Unexpected Infra Bottlenecks in Production AI

insights.claim: Doubleword bet four years ago that open-source models would win on cost, performance and privacy and that token cost would become a major scaling issue, and both predictions have been validated faster than anticipated.
insights.speaker: Meryem Arik
insights.so_what: Directional thesis on open-source inference and token economics is now consensus among operators; the surprise is pace, favoring infra that assumes rapid change.
insights.quote: We had two major predictions when we started the company four years ago. One is that open-source models would have to win...We had another bet that token cost would be a very big issue
insights.locator: Unexpected Infra Bottlenecks in Production AI

insights.claim: Uber exhausted its full annual AI budget in the first four months of 2026, at a per-user cost cited around $200-$500, after gamified leaderboard incentives encouraged employees to maximize AI usage.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Incentive design directly drives runaway AI spend; watch for enterprises pulling back gamified adoption programs and adding hard per-user caps.
insights.quote: Uber just recently published this in May that they ran out of budget for the yearly budget. They consumed it in the first four months of the year.
insights.locator: Measuring the AI Cost Per Dev

insights.claim: Per-employee token spend varies wildly and is justified by value, not absolute size: some NVIDIA-adjacent teams spend ~$15,000/month per member productively while other firms complain at $200/month, and Uber's cap was around $1,500.
insights.speaker: Meryem Arik
insights.so_what: Judge AI spend on value-capture per seat, not on dollar totals; the constraint is proving ROI, not limiting tokens.
insights.quote: their token spend for people in their team is like $15,000 a month for one team member, and they don't mind it because...it's improving our velocity
insights.locator: Measuring the AI Cost Per Dev

insights.claim: Agentic 'token maxing' — agents looping and retrying creatively to solve basic problems — inflates token usage and can turn a small product bug into a large bill, making governance and observability the real cost lever.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Cost control is an engineering/governance problem (bounding loops and retries), not a pricing negotiation; observability wrappers around agents become mandatory tooling.
insights.quote: they do a loop and then they try again, try again, try a different route. They are creative. With this creativity, there is more token usage
insights.locator: Measuring the AI Cost Per Dev

themes.theme: Physical Constraints: Compute, Energy, External Endpoints

insights.claim: Data-center energy availability, not just GPU supply or cost, now forces siting decisions to be planned years in advance around locations with sufficient power for AI workloads.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Energy access is a multi-year gating input for AI capacity; favors regions/utilities with power headroom and firms that pre-secure it.
insights.quote: if you want to actually have a data center, you have to plan years in advance because you have to think about locations where there's enough energy
insights.locator: Unexpected Infra Bottlenecks in Production AI

insights.claim: For teams running their own data centers, the first resource to run out is typically compute, not power, because agentic workloads spawn process-like sub-agents that keep running even after a parent agent dies.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: Orphaned sub-agent processes are a new source of compute leakage; Kubernetes-level lifecycle management of agents becomes a distinct discipline.
insights.quote: if an agent spins up a sub-agent, it can keep running even if the parent agent just dies for some reason. There are some parallels with operating systems there.
insights.locator: Unexpected Infra Bottlenecks in Production AI

insights.claim: The newest and hardest bottleneck when relying on external AI endpoints is unpredictable availability: responses can fail, arrive at 3-4x expected latency, or be truncated, with throttling appearing simply because another region (the US) woke up and hit the same constrained resource.
insights.speaker: Luca Bianchi
insights.so_what: External-endpoint variance is qualitatively worse than legacy DB scaling; time-of-day, cross-region contention must be designed around with retries, fallbacks and multi-provider routing.
insights.quote: The only difference was that a new region was awake. The U.S. were awake and they were starting to use the same endpoints and the same constrained resources and then we were throttled

insights.claim: There is simply not enough compute in the world to satisfy AI demand, so even best-in-class inference providers still hit noisy-neighbor slowdowns on multi-tenant endpoints.
insights.speaker: Meryem Arik
insights.so_what: Compute scarcity is structural, not transient; SLAs on shared inference endpoints will remain soft, rewarding workload-specific providers and capacity pre-commitment.
insights.quote: there just isn't enough compute in the world to satisfy the demand. Even the best capacity planning in the world and the best inference provider in the world still probably doesn't have enough compute.

insights.claim: Self-hosting inference at meaningful scale has become too big a job for most companies because it requires full capacity planning and building an entire inference stack, so it is best outsourced to specialized inference providers unless it is a full-time internal mandate.
insights.speaker: Meryem Arik
insights.so_what: Bullish for managed inference providers; the original open-source self-hosting rationale (privacy/control) is losing to operational burden except in regulated cases.
insights.quote: the job of inference has just become too big for most companies to even attempt to self-host at any decent scale

insights.claim: Regulated-sector customers self-hosting models struggle to plan six months ahead because both underlying hardware prices and model accuracy shift continuously, making it hard to commit to a specific model version like a given Qwen release.
insights.speaker: Luca Bianchi
insights.so_what: 100% data locality carries a real planning tax; hybrid architectures hedge the moving targets of price and model quality.
insights.quote: the price of the underlying hardware resources are constantly changing, but the other is that also the accuracy and the models are changing as well

insights.claim: A workable pattern for sensitive data is tiered models: local models handle non-anonymized sensitive data, an anonymization layer strips the most sensitive fields, and frontier models process the anonymized remainder to balance security, locality and cost.
insights.speaker: Luca Bianchi
insights.so_what: Anonymization-plus-frontier routing is the emerging compliance architecture; creates demand for PII-stripping middleware between local and frontier tiers.
insights.quote: an anonymization layer that could strip all the most sensitive data and then leverage on frontier models in order to be able to balance the shift between data security, data locality, and on the other side, cost

insights.claim: Latest open-source models such as GLM 5.2 have made large capability jumps and are much cheaper with good latency depending on the inference provider, so any team that hasn't tested them in 6-12 months is working from stale assumptions.
insights.speaker: Meryem Arik
insights.so_what: Open-source price/performance is moving fast enough to re-run build-vs-buy and model-selection decisions on a 6-month cycle.
insights.quote: try the latest open-source models. They have made huge capability improvements over the last months, like GLM 5.2. They are incredibly good, much cheaper.

themes.theme: Database as the Agentic Control Plane

insights.claim: In agentic AI the database has shifted from passive back-end repository to the control plane, storing agent telemetry, token usage, agent identity/metadata for access control and revocation, and audit logs.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Value accrues to databases that natively handle agent identity, auditing and telemetry; positions distributed SQL as agentic infrastructure, not commodity storage.
insights.quote: the database has become the control plane...you have to manage the identity and treat the agents with an identity to access what they have access to and revoke access to the agent

insights.claim: Agentic applications cannot tolerate eventual consistency because one agent acting on stale or wrong data can cascade errors through downstream agents that persist wrong records, making synchronized global consistency mandatory.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Strong-consistency distributed SQL is favored over eventually-consistent stores for multi-agent systems; a differentiator to underwrite.
insights.quote: You cannot allow for eventual consistency in these types of applications. They have to be consistent. Otherwise, your agents are going to act on wrong information.

insights.claim: Distributed SQL presenting many regional nodes as a single logical database lets agents in a location like Italy get local latencies while keeping data globally synchronized and consistent.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Geo-distributed single-logical-DB architecture is the pitch for agent latency plus consistency; relevant to CockroachDB-style vendors.
insights.quote: a database that is distributed across multiple regions in the world, but it's seen as a single logical database on top of it

insights.claim: A large credit-card provider is building agent rollback via two partners: DBOS to store and trace agent workflows in the database for step-by-step reversal, and Memori to persist agent memory state and inject prior learnings into new prompts using in-database vector search.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Names concrete vendors (DBOS, Memori) building the agent workflow/memory layer on top of databases — an emerging tooling category to track.
insights.quote: one called DBOS, and that's a product that basically helps track the workflow of the agents...The other company that we are working with is called Memori

insights.claim: Cockroach Labs is embedding AI agents inside the database itself to autonomously monitor stalling performance, slowing writes and rising latency, reviving the autonomous-database vision Oracle pitched in the early 2000s.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Self-tuning AI-in-database is the vendor response to DBAs being unable to keep up with agent load; a differentiation angle for infra databases.
insights.quote: we are working on making the database more intelligent. We are adding AI capabilities to our database. In real time, the AI agent can be monitoring, why is performance stalling?
insights.locator: Infrastructure - Wisdom from the Past, and the Present

insights.claim: Databases were historically designed for humans to retrieve and view data, but the emerging model is agents retrieving from many databases, which lowers the importance of keeping database count within human cognitive load.
insights.speaker: Luca Bianchi
insights.so_what: Agent-first data access could reverse decades of schema/consolidation norms optimized for human comprehension.
insights.quote: in the past, we used to plan a database for humans...Now the logics are changing. In the near future, we probably will have agents retrieving data from databases, maybe from a number of databases.
insights.locator: Infrastructure - Wisdom from the Past, and the Present

insights.claim: Rolling back autonomous agents is fundamentally harder than reverting a deployment because agent outputs include created files, config changes, opened pull requests, cloud API calls and triggered CI/CD workflows that are often irreversible, shifting the problem from reverting to compensating for side effects.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: The right investment is prevention — feature flags, dry-run, approval gates, idempotent operations — not after-the-fact rollback tooling.
insights.quote: rollback becomes less about reverting a deployment and more like compensating for side effects...the cleanest rollback is often just like preventing irreversible actions from happening automatically

insights.claim: You can roll back the effect of an agent's action (e.g., a database query) but not the reasoning process that led the agent to choose a given tool, introducing irreducible uncertainty in agentic systems.
insights.speaker: Luca Bianchi
insights.so_what: Guardrails and effect-level compensation, not reasoning replay, are the practical control surface for agent safety.
insights.quote: It is very difficult to roll back the reasoning process that led to that query or led to the usage of that tool.

insights.claim: Governance of what agents do and spend is a major unsolved layer: teams need observability wrappers around every deployed agent, and for Claude Code and Codex sessions telemetry and logs are already written to the local file system and can be accessed directly.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: Agent governance/observability is a greenfield category (Forge is building it); existing local telemetry from Claude Code/Codex lowers the barrier to naive in-house solutions.
insights.quote: for all Claude Code sessions, for all Codex sessions, this telemetry and the logs are literally getting stored on your file system. You can directly access them
insights.locator: Measuring the AI Cost Per Dev

insights.claim: A key governance risk is engineers pasting production code or API keys into models, requiring a control layer over exactly what data is fed into models — a problem robust enough that out-of-box solutions are being built rather than left to hacky in-house fixes.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: Data-egress/DLP for LLM prompts is a distinct security product opportunity beyond cost governance.
insights.quote: what if they're pasting a bunch of production code, or what if they're pasting in API keys? We need some sort of way to govern exactly what's getting fed into these models
insights.locator: Measuring the AI Cost Per Dev

insights.claim: The unit economics of software have changed radically: infrastructure is now a key or the key cost driver, ending the era of 70-80% software margins because inference/infra cost, not scalability, dominates the cost base.
insights.speaker: Meryem Arik
insights.so_what: Reprice AI-heavy software businesses on a lower gross-margin structure; SaaS-style multiples may not hold for inference-intensive products.
insights.quote: The days of 70%, 80% margins, it's just not possible anymore. Infrastructure is actually a key cost driver, or maybe the key cost driver in a business.
insights.locator: Infrastructure - Wisdom from the Past, and the Present

insights.claim: The old wisdom of 'just autoscale through demand spikes' is obsolete for AI agents because one user action can trigger a loop of model calls, tool calls, code execution, retries and DB writes, so autoscaling an inefficient loop amplifies the problem into a huge bill or outage instead of solving it.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: Bound agents (limit runtime, tool calls, retries, blast radius) before enabling elasticity; contrarian to default cloud-scaling playbooks.
insights.quote: the idea of if the demand spikes, then just autoscale your way through it, like obsolete...autoscaling doesn't actually solve the problem, it can actually amplify it
insights.locator: Infrastructure - Wisdom from the Past, and the Present

insights.claim: Capacity planning for expected growth is impossible under agentic AI because agent-generated workload is unpredictable, so architectures should be designed for elasticity rather than a forecasted load, ideally by separating compute from storage.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Compute/storage separation and elastic architecture are the hedge against unforecastable agent load; a design mandate and a vendor selling point.
insights.quote: database vendors like CockroachDB, what we're trying to do to minimize is separate compute from storage, that gives you the elasticity of compute
insights.locator: Infrastructure - Wisdom from the Past, and the Present

insights.claim: Teams face a two-sided capacity squeeze: their own AI workloads can trigger runaway bills via unbounded autoscaling (e.g., Karpenter spinning up nodes/pods on misconfiguration), while cloud providers impose soft and hard limits (e.g., on Bedrock) that are often too low to meet demand.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: Both over-provisioning (cost) and under-provisioning (capacity denial) hit simultaneously; guardrails plus multi-provider capacity are needed.
insights.quote: If you use Karpenter to generate more nodes, or just create more pods within your Kubernetes environments, you create more and more compute and containers running your programs for things that could be like misconfigurations
insights.locator: Infrastructure - Wisdom from the Past, and the Present

insights.claim: On-call SREs bear the hardest burden of AI infrastructure because production can break even when user traffic is normal: the same number of users can produce far more load when a prompt change, new tool call or larger context makes an agent loop more aggressively, spiking demand tenfold.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: Decoupling of user count from load breaks traditional capacity signals; SRE tooling must monitor per-request work, not just request count.
insights.quote: the same number of users can suddenly produce so much more traffic or load...It can increase your demand by tenfold for your infrastructure.

insights.claim: The database and its DBA/data-platform team remain the default culprit in agentic outages — paged at 2 a.m. when agent loops hammer the database, forced to migrate schemas across 40M+ rows when embeddings change, and told to build audit trails when security flags governance gaps.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Operational pain concentrates on data-platform teams; tooling that automates schema migration, online changes and agent audit trails targets a real bottleneck.
insights.quote: the database is always guilty until proven otherwise...the SRE team gets paged at 2 a.m. because the agent loop is hammering the database. Guess who is going to be in the call? It's the DBA.

insights.claim: The recommended first move is not a full architecture redesign but tracing one critical production AI workflow end-to-end (model calls, tool calls, DB queries) and asking where it is unbounded — max runtime, max tool calls, behavior under dependency slowdown or bad retry.
insights.speaker: Simerus Mahesh
insights.so_what: A concrete, low-cost diagnostic to find blast-radius risks before they cause bills or outages; find the unbounded paths first.
insights.quote: I think the goal is to find the places where the system is unbounded.

insights.claim: Practitioners should demand vendors demonstrate data-infrastructure behavior under failure rather than under the constrained TPC-C benchmark: online schema changes, node failure, network partition, region failure and in-place software updates.
insights.speaker: Alex Infanzon
insights.so_what: Failure-mode benchmarking becomes the real procurement test for AI-era databases because agents guarantee the system runs under pressure.
insights.quote: your vendor should show you now what happens if the network partitions, or what happens if a node goes down...The benchmarks, as of today, it's TPC-C, they are very constrained.

insights.claim: The durable practitioner strategy is building evolutionary, adaptive architecture that can absorb constant change, since new shared-compute databases, agents and models will keep being released.
insights.speaker: Luca Bianchi
insights.so_what: Optionality and adaptability beat point optimization in a fast-moving stack; a bias toward loosely coupled, swappable components.
insights.quote: The only answer that I could find is building an architecture that is able to evolve, that is able to adapt and use this new advancement as soon as they become available.
